## Definition

A foundational chapter in biochemistry covering the chemical and cellular basis of human physiology, including atomic structure, bonding, chemical reactions, energy, water chemistry, acid–base balance, and basic cell architecture.

## Core Principles

- The human body is composed of a defined set of main chemical elements, organised into atoms with characteristic atomic structure (nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons).
- Matter exists as atoms, ions (charged atoms), molecules (bonded atoms) and compounds (molecules of different elements).
- Chemical bonds form via ionic bonding (transfer of electrons) or covalent bonding (sharing of electrons).
- A chemical reaction involves the rearrangement of atoms; reactions are classified by their energy change as endergonic (energy-absorbing) or exergonic (energy-releasing).
- Energy is the capacity to do work, measured in defined units (e.g. joules, calories).
- ATP serves as the principal energy currency of the cell, coupling exergonic to endergonic processes.
- Common reaction types in metabolism include synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, and oxidation/reduction reactions.
- Water's polarity underpins its solvent properties and its physiological functions.
- Quantitative chemistry uses the mole, molar, and molarity to express amounts and concentrations.
- Acids, bases, and salts are defined by their behaviour in aqueous solution; the pH scale quantifies acidity and alkalinity.
- The cell is organised around the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, and mitochondria, each with distinct structural and functional roles.

## Sub-concepts

- Main chemical elements of the human body
- Atomic structure
- Atoms, ions, molecules, compounds
- Ionic vs covalent bonds
- Chemical reactions: endergonic vs exergonic
- Energy and its units
- ATP and cellular energy
- Reaction types: synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, oxidation/reduction
- Water as a solvent
- Mole, molar, molarity
- Acids, bases, salts
- pH scale; acidic and alkaline
- Basic cell structure: plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria

## Practical Applications

- Understanding ATP turnover provides the basis for analysing energy provision during sport and exercise.
- Acid–base balance and the pH scale underpin interpretation of metabolic and respiratory disturbances during exercise.
- Knowledge of molarity supports preparation of physiological solutions and interpretation of blood chemistry.
- Cellular structure (notably mitochondria) provides the framework for understanding aerobic energy production in skeletal muscle.
